titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the James Stevenson Papers
titlestmt.author: by Emma Gronbeck

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Tuesday October 4, 2022 at 03:01
Updated by Rosemary K. J. Davis to reflect donor restriction.

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: The James Stevenson Papers contains personal and literary correspondence, original artwork, writings, notes, personal papers, clippings, photographs, and audiovisual material documenting the life and career of American cartoonist and children's book author James Stevenson. The bulk of the collection consists of drafts of writings and illustrations for children's books, illustrated memoirs, and young adult novels, as well as cartoons, writings, and illustrations submitted to The New Yorker and The New York Times. Additional material includes proofs, correspondence, and financial and legal records related to publication of his works. The papers also contain drafts of his lesser known body of theatrical writings and early cartoons and illustrations he created for the student newspaper of the Hackley School. Correspondents include fellow illustrator Frank Modell, Judy Blume, Ethel Kennedy, and composer Gabriel Fontrier. The records span from 1902 to 2017, with the bulk of the material falling between 1945 and 2016.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.
Boxes 71, 153-154 are restricted until 2040. For further information consult the appropriate curator.
Box 147 (computer disks): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
Box 148 (audiovisual material): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
Box 149 (record album storage): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into six series: I. Correspondence, 1962-2017. II. Writings and Illustrations, 1907-2017. III. The New Yorker , 1956-2016. IV. Visual Materials, circa 1950-2016. V. Personal Papers, 1902-2017. VI. Computer and Audiovisual Media, 1986-2016.

bioghist.head: James Stevenson (1929-2017)
bioghist.p: James Stevenson (Yale 1951) was an American illustrator and writer. He worked as a cartoonist for The New Yorker from 1956 to 2003 and was the author and illustrator of more than 100 children's picture books. Stevenson was born on July 11, 1929 in New York City to architect Harvey Stevenson and Winifred Worcester and attended the private Hackley School in Tarrytown, New York. He received a B.A. in English from Yale in 1951, where he worked on the campus humor magazine The Yale Record . While a student at Yale, he submitted cartoon ideas and sketches to The New Yorker . He was hired by the magazine in 1956 to develop ideas for the art department before starting to draw cartoons himself. During his 47-year career at The New Yorker , he published 1,988 cartoons and 79 magazine covers. He also began working as a reporter in 1960 and continued to contribute articles and essays, such as pieces for "The Talk of the Town" section, after retiring from the magazine in 2003. He previously worked as a reporter for Life from 1953 to 1954, and later wrote the column "Lost and Found New York" for The New York Times from 2003 to 2009.

Stevenson published his first children's book, Walker, the Witch, and the Striped Flying Saucer , in 1969. One of his earliest books, If I Owned a Candy Factory , was written in collaboration with his young son James in 1968. Other titles include The Worst Person in the World (1978), Don't You Know There's a War On? (1992), and No Laughing, No Giggling, No Smiling (2004). His books What's Under My Bed? (1983) and Could Be Worse! (1987) were featured on the PBS Reading Rainbow television show. Could Be Worse! was also selected as The New York Times Outstanding Children's Book of the Year. In addition to his many picture books, Stevenson published adult and young adult novels, several illustrated collections of his poetry, and an illustrated biography of fellow New Yorker cartoonist Frank Modell. He also illustrated books for authors such as Judy Blume, Dr. Seuss, Jack Prelutsky, Cynthia Rylant, and Alan Arkin. Stevenson married Jane Walker in 1953, and later painter Josephine Merck in 1993. He was the father of nine children and a longtime resident of Cos Cob, Connecticut, where he died on February 17, 2017.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: James Stevenson Papers. General Collection, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.

Information included in the Description of Papers note and Collection Contents section is drawn from information supplied with the collection and from an initial survey of the contents. Folder titles appearing in the contents list below are often based on those provided by the creator or previous custodian. Titles have not been verified against the contents of the folders in all cases. Otherwise, folder titles are supplied by staff during initial processing.
This finding aid may be updated periodically to account for new acquisitions to the collection and/or revisions in arrangement and description.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The James Stevenson Papers contains personal and literary correspondence, original artwork, writings, notes, personal papers, clippings, photographs, and audiovisual material documenting the life and career of American cartoonist and children's book author James Stevenson. The bulk of the collection consists of drafts of writings and illustrations for children's books, illustrated memoirs, and young adult novels, as well as cartoons, writings, and illustrations submitted to The New Yorker and The New York Times. Additional material includes proofs, correspondence, and financial and legal records related to publication of his works. The papers also contain drafts of his lesser known body of theatrical writings and early cartoons and illustrations he created for the student newspaper of the Hackley School. Correspondents include fellow illustrator Frank Modell, Judy Blume, Ethel Kennedy, and composer Gabriel Fontrier. The records span from 1902 to 2017, with the bulk of the material falling between 1945 and 2016.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The James Stevenson papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized alphabetically by surname or corporate name, followed by unsorted correspondence, fan mail, and letters of condolence.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Correspondence can also be found in Series II. Writings and Illustrations, Series III. The New Yorker , and Series V. Personal Papers.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series primarily contains incoming personal and professional correspondence.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Correspondence can also be found in Series II. Writings and Illustrations, Series III. The New Yorker , and Series V. Personal Papers.

did.unittitle: Letters of condolence to Josie Merck
Letters of condolence to Josie Merck
Letters of condolence to Josie Merck
Letters of condolence to Josie Merck
Letters of condolence to Josie Merck

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains extensive drafts, artwork, and other materials associated with children's books, illustrated memoirs, theatrical and musical works, short writings, and comics by James Stevenson. The series also includes submissions to newspapers and magazines other than The New Yorker , such as The New York Times and The Washington Star .

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into six subseries: Illustrated Books and Comics, Novels, Musicals and Plays, The New York Times , Other Writings and Illustrations, and Notes and Research Materials.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains reference and research material, drafts, sketches and original illustrations, proofs, book reviews, publishing contracts, and correspondence related to illustrated children's books, poetry anthologies, long-form comics, and illustrated memoirs by Stevenson. Among the most well-documented works in this series are his early children's book Here Comes Herb's Hurricane! (1973), the "Hubie" series of books ( The Seaview Hotel , The Castaway , The Stowaway , and All Aboard! ), The Supreme Souvenir Factory , Bats Can't Bat , and the illustrated memoir The Dawn of the Sea Monster and Other Wonders of Block Island . Some books are written under Stevenson's pseudonym, Jake Wolf. Also included are files for books Stevenson illustrated for other authors, such as Judy Blume and Jack Prelutsky.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized alphabetically by title. Drafts with working titles are organized under the published title, or what appears to be the final title.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains early notes and outlines, drafts, proofs, and correspondence related to Stevenson's small collection of long-form prose works. Materials are primarily related to Stevenson's three young adult novels, The Bones in the Cliff , The Unprotected Witness , and Runaway Horse! , and a book entitled The Underwater Railroad .

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized alphabetically by title. Drafts with working titles are arranged under their published title, or what appears to be their final title.

did.unittitle: Printout from digital file, corrected, with correspondence
Holograph and printout from digital file, corrected
"Rootie II - en route / Lo Jack," drafts, holograph and typescript, corrected
"Rootie II," drafts, holograph and typescript, corrected, partial

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains notes and outlines, drafts of scripts, associated illustrations, correspondence with editors and performance centers, and original musical scores written by collaborating composers. A lesser-known area of Stevenson's creative work, his theatrical writings consist primarily of satirical comedies, such as Rolling in Dough , The Fiend in the Fog , and Evening at Anaheim , as well as a few works of historical fiction, such as Bingo Barker and the Great East Texas Oil Strike and Orville and Wilbur . Also included are drafts of Stevenson's first theatrical work, an operatic drama entitled Everyman , on which he collaborated in the early 1950s with his former Hackley School teacher, Gabriel Fontrier. Stevenson wrote the story and libretto while Fontrier composed the score. There are also notes and correspondence related to several of his musicals that were performed for live audiences, including Rolling in Dough and How the Century Created Santa Claus .

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized alphabetically by title. Drafts with working titles are organized under what is assumed to be the final title.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains material related to Stevenson's work as an op-ed contributor for The New York Times , primarily research materials, notes, drafts, illustrations, proofs, and clippings from his column "Lost and Found New York," which ran from 2003 to 2009 and included pieces on notable New York City politicians, celebrities, landmarks, and events.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized alphabetically by title. Drafts with working titles are arranged under the published title, or what is assumed to be the final title.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains research materials, notes, drafts, and associated sketches and illustrations of independent or unidentified short writings and comics, as well as cartoons, illustrations, and writings for publications such as Land's End , Preservation Magazine , and The Washington Star . Included are autobiographical writings potentially belonging to Stevenson's genealogical project "Stops on the Stevenson Line," a possible unfinished memoir, or another autobiographical work. There is also a small amount of writings by others, including Stevenson's daughter Suçie Stevenson, his brother Eric Stevenson, and his father-in-law Charles H. Walker.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized alphabetically by title or publication, with untitled works and writings of others filed at the end of the sequence. Drafts with working titles are arranged under the published title or what is assumed to be the final title.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains notebooks, journals, files of general or topical notes, newspaper clippings, articles, and reference photographs. Materials may correspond to works in other series or subseries, such as notebooks which contain notes for Stevenson's New Yorker profiles. Many notes files are unsorted and unidentified.

did.unittitle: Journal, "Drive to Las Vegas with Walker"
Notes, Century Association, address to new members
Notes and illustrations, "The Newport Folk Festival"
Reference material, "Capitol building, senate, house, offices," photographs and clippings

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series contains writings, illustrations, and cartoons created by Stevenson during his employment at The New Yorker or submitted as freelance work to The New Yorker .

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two subseries: Cartoons and Cover Illustrations, and Writings and Illustrations.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains original pencil and ink drawings of cartoons submitted for publication in The New Yorker , and original watercolor, crayon, and oil pastel paintings and illustrations submitted as issue covers for The New Yorker . Only those cover illustrations clearly marked as New Yorker submissions are included. There are also clippings of published cartoons and covers.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Illustrations which may have been submitted as covers for The New Yorker can also be found in Series IV. Visual Material.

did.unittitle: Cartoon original, "I can't quite picture a person who would enjoy a gift in that price range"
Cartoon original, "Nassau was impossible, Barbados was touristy…"
Cartoon original, "Stick to the script, Larry"
Cartoon original, "This is one of the worst Sundays so far"
Cartoon original, "We've just been acquired by Burger Kwik!"
Cartoon originals "R.G. May 22, 1963; seen by J.G. Jan 28, 1963"
Cartoon originals "R.G. May 17, seen by J.G. Jan 8, 1963"
Cartoon originals "seen by J.G. Jan 28, 1963; R.G. May 22, 1963"
Cartoon originals "R.G. Jan 29; seen by J.G. Mar 18, 1963"
Cartoon originals "R.G. Jan 29; seen by J.G. Mar 18, 1963; seen by J.B.M. Nov 4, 1964"
Cartoon originals, submissions and notes with correspondence
Cartoon and illustration clippings with sizing notes
Cover illustration original "raining cats and dogs"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains materials related to writings and illustrations Stevenson published in The New Yorker , including his profiles on notable people in the arts and humanities, such as John Carpenter, Pat Oliphant, and Son Seals. Stevenson's contributions also include nonfiction articles about Robert F. Kennedy, "Talk of the Town" columns, short fiction, short comics, and illustrations. Materials include notes, typescript drafts, original artwork, proofs, clippings, and related correspondence.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized alphabetically by title. Untitled and unsorted works are filed at the end of the sequence, with untitled works possessing an identification number filed first.

did.unittitle: "1973 Super-Inauguration Bowl Souvenir Program," illustrated holograph
"Adventures of the Mind and Spirit," illustrated holograph
"Album from the Attic II - The Family" (AE 7162), illustrations (killed)
"Annals of Law and Order," illustrated holograph
"Architect's Conception of Proposed New Plant," illustrated holograph
"The Book That Has Washington Talking," illustrated holograph
"The Breakwater; Going Out; The Event; The Return," typescript, corrected
"Brigs 7, Phoons 0, In Quickie," typescript, corrected, and page proofs
Research material, Bulletin of the University of Southern California School of Performing Arts 1977-78
"The Case Against Professor Lamberti," illustrated holograph
"The Cooley House-Webster Letters," typescript with illustrations
"Decide Your Way to Happiness," typescript with illustrations
"The Delsarte Method for Becoming President," illustrated holograph (killed)
"Dr. Svenblad Answers Your Personal Questions," typescript, corrected
"For Whom the Bell Tolls," illustrated holograph
"Forgotten Hero of American Science and Technology," illustrations
"The Glittering Days of Global Pictures," illustrated holograph
"The Golden Age of Malfeasance," illustrated holograph
"I Don't Want Beautiful," research material and notes on John Cassavetes
"If Part of the Way Through You Stopped," illustrated holograph
"If the President's Half-Hour Press Conference Had Been Allowed to Go Beyond Half-an-Hour…" typescript, corrected
"Illusions, Wonders Plus Disappearing Acts," illustrated holograph
"In the Drug Store They Have Everything," illustrated holograph
"The Last Waltz Clog, The First Carambola"
"Looking for Skunk Hollow," typescript, corrected, with notes
"Memo on Unicity," illustrated holograph with correspondence
"National Tennis Hall of Fame," illustrated holograph
"Notes from a Bottle," typescript, corrected, and clipping
"On Labor Day We Honor the Working Man and His Work," illustrations and page proofs
"Organizational Set-Up at White House," illustrated holograph
"The Phantom of the Studios," typescript, corrected
"Phys Ed at Kersey," typescript with illustrations
"The Red Wing Peanut Butter Company," typescript
"A Sneak Peek at the DNC," typescript, corrected
"Some Notes for a Study," illustrated holograph (killed)
"The Sound of Warren Foster," illustrated holograph
"The Visit to the Office," illustrated holograph
Holograph and typescript, corrected, with notes
"What to Do on a Saturday," illustrations
"Why Has My Electric Bill Gone Up?" typescript
"Why the International Hotel-Telegraph Code Was Abandoned," illustrated holograph
"Why I Am an Unsuccessful Artist," notes and correspondence
"Why I Am an Unsuccessful Artist," typescript, corrected

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Illustrated essay by Stevenson sent to New Yorker editor William Shawn criticizing The New Yorker 's graphic design choices.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains works of art on paper or canvas not identified as part of a written or published work, as well as the majority of photographic materials in the collection.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into three subseries: Drawings and Paintings, Sketches and Sketchbooks, and Photographs.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains primarily ink and watercolor paintings, though some works include other media such as wax crayon, oil pastel, charcoal, and gouache paint. Some works are accompanied by earlier drafts or reference materials. The majority of works in this subseries appear to be submissions or drafts for The New Yorker , but feature no identifying notes or documentation.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized alphabetically by subject, with additional files of illustrations titled by the creator, matted works, and unsorted works filed at the end of the sequence.

did.unittitle: "In the Office, In the Evening," illustration, matted

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains bound sketchbooks and folders of loose sketches and illustrations, many of which are unsorted and undated.

did.unittitle: Illustrations and sketches, "New York City parks and people"
Illustrations and sketches, "Newport, Rhode Island and Block Island"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains photographic prints, film negatives, print negatives, and contact sheets. Included are photographs of Stevenson's friends and family, a collection of photographs documenting Stevenson's offices at The New Yorker during the late 1970s and early 1980s, press photographs for Stevenson's publications, and photographs of Stevenson attending professional events.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Photographs can also be found in Series II. Writings and Illustrations.

did.unittitle: The Foundry in Guilford, Connecticut, black-and-white photographs
New York City, contact sheets (roll #15565-1 and #15565-2)
New York City, contact sheets (rolls #16240-1 and #16240-2)
New York City, contact sheets (rolls #16478-1 and #16478-2)
New Yorker offices and New York City, contact sheets (rolls #24079-1 and #24079-2)
New Yorker offices and people, photographs and writings
" New Yorker Photographs, 1978-1985, Vol. I," black-and-white photographs with captions (1 of 2)
" New Yorker Photographs, 1978-1985, Vol. I," black-and-white photographs with captions (2 of 2)
" New Yorker Photographs, 1978-1985 (+ 1987), Vol. 2," black-and-white photographs with captions
Stevenson, James - contact sheet by Danny Lindley (damaged)
Stevenson, James - black-and-white photographs by Anne Hall
Stevenson, James - photographs by Annie Watson
Stevenson, James, with his mother Winifred Worcester - photographs

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains materials related to Stevenson's personal life and family including awards; records from Stevenson's time at the Hackley School, where he contributed cartoons and illustrations to the student newspaper The Dial ; genealogical research; ephemera related to family deaths and memorials; school records and drawings from Stevenson's children; clippings about Stevenson; drawings and assorted ephemera.

did.unittitle: Stevenson, Winifred Worcester - memorial pamphlet, draft
Stevenson, Winifred Worcester - "Grandma Biffy memorabilia from Brearley School, 1917"
Stevenson, Winifred Worcester - "Grandma Biffy memorial" notes
Stevenson, Winifred Worcester - letters of condolence to James Stevenson
Stevenson, Winifred Worcester - memorial pamphlet, drafts and notes
Wedding announcement for James Stevenson and Josie Merck
Wedding napkins, James Stevenson and Josie Merck
The Coffee House Centennial, 1915-2015 publication (with DVD-R, "A Place at the Table")
Event invitations, "In honor of Naomi King and Peter Stevenson"
Exhibition of artwork, The Coffee House, New York City - correspondence and notes
Fontrier, Gabriel - memorial notes and ephemera
Fontrier, Gabriel - writings and compositions with correspondence
Framed musical score, "Smiles" by J. Will Callahan and Lee S. Roberts
Greenwich Early Risers, notes and ephemera
Hackley School, "A Christmas Carol" radio adaptation typescripts with annotations (12 copies)
Hackley School, "Notes for the Faculty of Hackley School, 1946-1947" (signed G. Fontrier)
Hackley School, "The Old Corker" illustrated parody magazine
Hackley School, "Paint It Red!" typescript, corrected
Hackley School, poem holographs and sketches
Hackley School, "Take It or Leave It" student newsletter
Memorabilia, "Souvenir of Fall River Line" booklet
Modell, Frank - birthday card from James Stevenson
The Old Black Point Association, pamphlets and ephemera
"The Old Black Point House Tour," informational packet
Poetry Foundation, Children's Poet Laureate award ephemera (for Jack Prelutsky books)
Stevenson family, notes and genealogy research
Stevenson, James - memorial notes and ephemera

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains rewritable computer disks, audiocassettes, 10" vinyl records, and published filmstrips.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two subseries: Computer Media, and Audiovisual Media.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Box 147 (computer disks): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
Box 148 (audiovisual media): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
Box 149 (record album storage): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains six rewritable or limited-release computer disks, including non-commercially produced recordings of songs from Stevenson's musical Rolling in Dough , and one limited-release DVD enclosed in a publication.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains ten audiocassettes, two 10" vinyl records, and two copies of a filmstrip and audiocassette edition of Stevenson's book The Oldest Elf .

did.unittitle: Brown, Cleo, "Pinetop's Boogie Woogie" / Ammons, Albert "Boogie Woogie Stomp," 10" vinyl record
The Fiend in the Fog , music audiocassette "For Jim S."
Johnson, Pete, "Blues on the Down Beat / Kaycee on My Mind," 10" vinyl record
The Night After Christmas: A filmstrip dramatization , 35mm filmstrip and audiocassette (published by Random House )
Rolling in Dough , audiocassette with full song list (17 tracks)

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Restricted until 2040. For further information consult the appropriate curator.

did.unittitle: Letters of condolence to Josie Merck from James Stevenson's family
